---
title: "The Corpus"
---


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary

The system has read six public documents — three UK regulator
supervisory statements, one EU regulator guideline, and two large
reinsurers' sustainability reports — and converted them into 461
small searchable pieces ("chunks"). This section describes which
documents were chosen and why, how they were converted from PDF to
text, how they were split into chunks, and how metadata about each
document (who published it, when, on what topic) is tracked. Three
additional fictional internal-style documents have been drafted to
show the system extends beyond regulators and listed firms, but are
not yet indexed into the searchable store.
:::

---

## What Is in the Corpus {#sec-data-corpus}

The indexed v1 corpus is **six PDF documents from four issuers**,
all publicly published, totalling 15.3 MB on disk and 461 chunks
after preprocessing. Their metadata is hand-curated in
`corpus/corpus_metadata.toml` and validated by a Pydantic schema at
load time (per D006).

| Document | Issuer | Type | Effective | Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Supervisory Statement 5/25 — Climate-related risks (banks and insurers) | PRA | Supervisory statement | 2025-12-01 | 588 KB |
| Supervisory Statement 3/19 — Financial risks from climate change | PRA | Supervisory statement (superseded by SS5/25) | 2024-11-01 | 1.16 MB |
| Supervisory Statement 1/21 — Operational resilience: Impact tolerances | PRA | Supervisory statement | 2021-03-01 | 2.39 MB |
| Guidelines on System of Governance | EIOPA | Regulatory guideline | 2014-01-01 | 264 KB |
| Sustainability Report 2023 | Munich Re | Sustainability report | 2023-12-31 | 5.05 MB |
| Sustainability Report 2024 | Swiss Re | Sustainability report | 2024-12-31 | 5.91 MB |

: The indexed v1 corpus (`corpus/real/`). {#tbl-data-corpus}

The three regulatory documents (PRA × 3, EIOPA × 1) cover the
**risk-management and governance** axis that an underwriter working
on UK or EEA business would routinely cite. The two reinsurer
sustainability reports (Munich Re, Swiss Re) cover the **disclosed
position** axis — what peer reinsurers have publicly committed to
on climate, thermal coal exclusions, oil-and-gas underwriting, and
related ESG topics. Together they exercise the system on the two
practical document modes an underwriter actually works against:
regulator-imposed *obligations* and competitor-published
*positions*.

### Why these documents

The corpus was deliberately kept small (six documents) so that the
evaluation could be hand-crafted, the citation gold could be
verified by hand, and the retrieval failures could be diagnosed
individually rather than statistically. A larger corpus is a v2
work item, not a v1 success criterion.

Within that constraint, the documents were chosen to satisfy three
properties:

- **Realism over breadth.** Each document is one an actual
  reinsurance underwriter would plausibly consult on a real
  question. Toy documents (e.g. Wikipedia abstracts on
  reinsurance) were rejected on Day 1.

- **Variety of structural shape.** The PRA supervisory statements
  have numbered paragraphs and tight section hierarchy; EIOPA's
  guidelines are similarly structured but use a different
  conventions; Munich Re and Swiss Re sustainability reports are
  designer-laid-out PDFs with figures, tables, and an order in
  which Docling does not always recover the reading sequence
  cleanly. The chunker (D008, below) is built to handle all
  three styles.

- **Variety of citation density.** A question against PRA SS5/25
  typically resolves to one specific paragraph (e.g. 4.124); a
  question against Munich Re's sustainability report may need to
  cite the prose plus an adjacent table cell; a cross-document
  question against Munich Re and Swiss Re needs to cite a
  paragraph from each. The benchmark exercises all three.

### Provenance note

The Munich Re Sustainability Report 2023 was retrieved from the
**UNEP FI mirror** after the canonical `munichre.com` URL began
returning a 404 on 2026-06-17. The `corpus_metadata.toml`
`source_url` field records the URL actually used for retrieval,
not the canonical publisher URL, on the principle that a reviewer
needs to know *where the document in the indexed corpus came from*,
not where it ideally should have been retrieved from. This is the
discipline the project applies across its decision logs and
journal: record the world as it actually was on the day of the
decision, not a tidied-up version after the fact.

---

## Document Conversion: Docling with OCR Disabled {#sec-data-docling}

PDF → markdown conversion is handled by [Docling](https://github.com/DS4SD/docling),
configured with OCR explicitly **disabled** per D004.

The default Docling pipeline runs OCR on every page of every PDF
regardless of whether the PDF actually contains scanned images. For
text-PDF corpora (verified via the `file` command), this means
roughly 40 MB of OCR model weights downloaded on first run and
roughly 60% additional ingestion time per document — for zero
quality improvement, because the underlying text layer is already
clean. All six documents in the v1 corpus are text PDFs, so OCR
runs only on the (currently empty) set of corpus documents that
would actually need it.

The mitigation is documented in D004 alongside the decision: if a
future scanned PDF is added to the corpus, OCR must be re-enabled
for that specific document via a per-document configuration
override. Silent fallback to OCR-off-for-everything is preferred to
silent fallback to OCR-on-for-everything, on the basis that the
former produces an obvious empty-text symptom and the latter
produces an opaque 60%-slower ingest with no signal.

Reinsurer sustainability reports dominate ingestion time. The two
sustainability reports (Munich Re + Swiss Re) together account for
roughly **80% of the ~134-second full-corpus Docling pass**, despite
being only 33% of the document count. Their internal layout (figures,
tables, multi-column passages) is structurally more expensive to
extract than the linear paragraph flow of the supervisory
statements. Any chunker or cleanup tuning is therefore batched
rather than re-ingesting on every change.

---

## Cleanup Pre-Pass {#sec-data-cleanup}

After Docling produces markdown, a three-rule cleanup pre-pass
addresses systematic extraction artefacts. The rules are applied in
order:

1. **Universal: strip `<!-- image -->` placeholders.** Docling emits
   one of these for every image it detects but does not extract;
   they appear 398 times across the v1 corpus and are pure noise
   for retrieval. Removed corpus-wide.

2. **Structural: dedupe repeated table-of-contents blocks.** Munich
   Re's sustainability report contains, in the PDF, a top-level TOC
   that Docling extracts verbatim three or more times due to
   recurring section-header tables. The cleanup pass keeps the
   first occurrence and removes subsequent verbatim duplicates,
   preserving distinct TOC variants (which do exist in the source
   PDF) by keying on exact text equality, not by counting.

3. **Document-specific: targeted glyph and watermark fixes.** EIOPA's
   PDF contains a recurrent `glyph[.notdef]` artefact from a font-
   encoding pathology, mapped to a hyphen. PRA SS1/21 carries an
   inline "Superseded" watermark and a "Please see: ss1/22-march-2022.pdf"
   link that appears mid-paragraph; both are stripped because they
   are not actually part of the document's text content.

The cleanup function is pure — input string + document_id, output
cleaned string + statistics. No I/O, no global state, fully
testable.

---

## Chunking: Two-Pass with Floor Merge {#sec-data-chunking}

Chunking is the single most consequential preprocessing decision
because it determines both what the dense embeddings index and what
the answer model receives as context. The v1 chunker is the result
of D008 (which superseded an earlier D007 single-mode strategy)
and uses a **two-pass algorithm** with explicit token bounds.

### Pass 1 — Emission

The markdown is parsed at heading boundaries (`##`, `###`, etc.).
Each segment between two headings becomes a candidate chunk,
including a `(preamble)` segment for any text before the first
heading. For each candidate:

- If the segment fits under a **soft cap of 1500 tokens**, it is
  emitted as a single `hierarchy` chunk preserving its
  section-path metadata.

- If the segment exceeds the soft cap, it is split using paragraph
  fallback: numbered-paragraph anchors first (PRA-style "4.124"
  markers), then blank-line paragraphs, then greedy word splitting
  as a last resort.

### Pass 2 — Floor merge

Sub-floor chunks (fewer than **100 tokens**) are merged with their
neighbours to avoid stranded fragments. The merge prefers backward
(into the previous chunk), falling back to forward (into the next)
if backward would push the receiver over the cap. If either merge
would breach the cap, the sub-floor chunk is left standing.

The loop does not advance after a merge — the same position is
rechecked, so a merged chunk that is itself still under floor gets
re-merged in a subsequent iteration. This handles the case where
several adjacent small fragments need to be coalesced together.

### Output

The full corpus produces **461 chunks**, all between 100 and 1500
tokens. Chunk IDs are stable across runs (the emission pass
numbers chunks sequentially; the floor-merge pass leaves gaps in
the ID sequence rather than renumbering). This stability matters
for the citation contract: a `[chunk_id]` in an answer should
resolve to the same paragraph today and after the next corpus
rebuild.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Token counts are word splits, not tokenizer tokens
The 1500/100 thresholds are in units of **whitespace-split words**,
not BGE-M3 or model-family BPE/WordPiece tokens. The Probe-03
size-distribution analysis that calibrated these thresholds used
word counts; swapping in a real tokenizer would silently change
what the thresholds mean. If the embedding model is ever changed,
D008 must be revisited.
:::

---

## Metadata: Hand-Curated TOML, Pydantic-Validated {#sec-data-metadata}

Per-document metadata is stored in `corpus/corpus_metadata.toml` and
validated at load time against the `DocumentMetadata` Pydantic
schema in `src/underwriting_copilot/metadata.py`. The decision to
hand-curate metadata rather than infer it from document content is
recorded as D006.

The fields tracked per document, all inherited by every chunk via
the chunk's `document_id`, are:

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `document_id` | string | Stable id used by retrieval filters and citation IDs |
| `title` | string | Display name used in source cards and citations |
| `document_type` | enum | `supervisory_statement` / `regulatory_guideline` / `sustainability_report` / `internal_policy` |
| `issuer` | string | Publishing organisation (`PRA`, `EIOPA`, `Munich Re`, ...) |
| `issuer_type` | enum | `regulator` / `reinsurer` / `internal` |
| `jurisdiction` | string | `UK` / `EU` / `Global` / etc. — used in retrieval filters |
| `effective_date` | date | Used to detect supersession and currency |
| `version` | string | Document version identifier (`SS5/25`, `2024`, ...) |
| `superseded_by` | string? | Optional pointer to a newer document_id |
| `topics` | list[string] | Free-form topic labels (see *Q5* below) |
| `provenance` | enum | `real` / `synthetic` |
| `source_url` | URL | Where the document was actually retrieved (not necessarily the canonical publisher URL) |

The two cleanly-typed fields (`document_type` and `issuer_type`)
were promoted from free-text to enum after the first hand-curated
pass surfaced a small number of typos and inconsistent
capitalisation; the Pydantic schema catches new typos on the next
metadata edit.

### Supersession is tracked structurally {#sec-data-supersession}

The `superseded_by` field is non-cosmetic. PRA SS3/19 (climate
financial risks, effective 2024) was superseded in late 2025 by
PRA SS5/25 (the same topic, expanded scope). Both PDFs are in the
indexed corpus because both remained in the wild for the first
half of 2026 and an underwriter researching a question that
existed under SS3/19 may still need to know what it said.

The retrieval pipeline exposes an `exclude_superseded=True`
parameter, defaulted to True, which filters SS3/19 chunks out of
the candidate pool whenever its successor SS5/25 is in the corpus.
The decision to default this to True (rather than letting both
versions compete in retrieval) was D012, on the basis that the
default behaviour of any production research tool should be to
return the currently-in-force version unless explicitly asked
otherwise.

A user who specifically needs the SS3/19 perspective can override
the flag at retrieval time. The default protects the common case.

### Topic vocabulary remains open (Q5)

The `topics` field is free-form across the v1 corpus, and the
first hand-curated pass surfaced near-synonyms that have not been
resolved: `scenario_analysis` (used in PRA climate documents) vs
`scenario_testing` (used in PRA SS1/21 on operational resilience);
`esg` vs `sustainability` across the two reinsurer reports. For
*retrieval*, hybrid retrieval handles synonyms gracefully — both
terms surface relevant chunks. For *filtering* — e.g. "show me
only ESG documents" — the synonym pair splits the result set.

Q5 in `docs/open_questions.md` records the deferral: whether to
introduce a controlled vocabulary now (six documents, easy) or
defer until the corpus is larger and the synonym problem is
visible in eval results. The defer-with-reasoning posture is
recorded explicitly; the question stays open rather than being
quietly decided.

---

## Indexing: Dense + Sparse, Both Local {#sec-data-indexing}

The 461 chunks are indexed twice, both stores running locally with
no remote services in the pipeline.

**Dense index** — BGE-M3 embeddings produced via `mlx-embeddings`
on Apple Silicon (D009 path B). Embeddings are CLS-pooled and
L2-normalised (D010) and stored in a local Qdrant collection at
`scratch/qdrant/`. The model was selected for its strong
multilingual and domain coverage; regulatory English and corporate-
disclosure English are both well within its training distribution.

**Sparse index** — BM25 with a custom Porter-stemmed vocabulary
extracted from the corpus, persisted at `corpus/bm25_vocab.json`.
The vocabulary contains roughly 4,810 stem-normalised terms after
stopword filtering. BM25 was implemented in-repo rather than
imported from a heavy IR library, both to keep dependencies small
and to make the scoring fully inspectable when retrieval failures
need to be diagnosed by hand.

Both indices are built once and persisted to disk. The retrieval
pipeline (Section 4) queries both at every request and fuses the
results.

The architectural decision to use **both** retrieval channels —
rather than dense alone — was made on Day 1 evidence and recorded
as D009. The reasoning is in the next section.

---

## Synthetic Documents: Drafted, Not Indexed {#sec-data-synthetic}

Three additional synthetic documents are present on disk in
`corpus/synthetic/`, representing the kinds of internal documents
an actual deployment would also need to handle:

| Filename | Document type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `sycamore_risk_appetite_statement.md` | Risk appetite statement | Board-level statement of tolerable risk |
| `sycamore_delegated_authority_matrix.md` | Delegated authority matrix | Who can underwrite what, up to what limits |
| `sycamore_thermal_coal_underwriting_policy.md` | Internal underwriting policy | Issuer-specific exclusions and conditions |

These documents are written under the fictional issuer **Sycamore
Reinsurance** (clearly labelled as synthetic in the metadata
schema's `provenance = "synthetic"` field) to demonstrate
structurally that the pipeline extends to internal-category
content without requiring any real reinsurer to share confidential
documents for an interview artefact.

In v1, however, these three documents are **drafted but not
indexed**. They do not appear in the BM25 vocabulary, they are not
in the Qdrant collection, and the v1 eval numbers are reported
exclusively against the 4-issuer real corpus. Indexing the
synthetic documents is a half-day v2 task and is recorded as a v2
work item rather than half-finished in v1.

The reason for the carve-out is honest accounting: if the synthetic
documents were partially indexed, eval numbers would be reported
against a corpus that does not exist for any of the questions
written before the synthetic documents were drafted, and the
benchmark would need to be re-checked end-to-end to preserve
gold-chunk references. Within the five-day budget, drafting the
synthetic documents (to show the pipeline scope) is preferred to
indexing them (which would require a benchmark refresh that does
not fit).

---

## Corpus at a Glance {#sec-data-glance}

| | |
|---|---|
| **Indexed documents** | 6 |
| **Indexed issuers** | 4 (PRA × 3, EIOPA × 1, Munich Re × 1, Swiss Re × 1) |
| **Document types** | Supervisory statement (×3), regulatory guideline (×1), sustainability report (×2) |
| **Jurisdictions** | UK (×3), EU (×1), Global (×2) |
| **Total disk size** | 15.3 MB across 6 PDFs |
| **Chunks after preprocessing** | **461**, all 100–1500 tokens |
| **PDF → markdown** | Docling, OCR disabled (D004) |
| **Cleanup rules** | 3 (image-placeholder strip, TOC dedupe, document-specific glyph/watermark) |
| **Chunker** | Two-pass per D008: emission with paragraph fallback + floor-merge |
| **Metadata** | Hand-curated TOML, Pydantic-validated (D006) |
| **Supersession** | Tracked structurally; SS3/19 → SS5/25 filtered by default (D012) |
| **Dense index** | BGE-M3 (CLS-pooled, L2-normalised), Qdrant local |
| **Sparse index** | BM25 with Porter-stemmed vocabulary (~4,810 terms) |
| **Drafted, not indexed** | 3 synthetic Sycamore Reinsurance documents (v2 work) |
| **Topic vocabulary** | Open (Q5) — free-form labels, controlled-vocab decision deferred |
